title: Crossings and Nestings of Matchings and Partitions
overall_summary: The paper proves that, for set partitions and in particular for complete matchings, the maximum size of a crossing family and the maximum size of a nesting family have a symmetric joint distribution, even after fixing the sets of minimal and maximal block elements. The core technique is a bijection between set partitions and vacillating tableaux built from RSK row insertion and deletion; under this bijection, the crossing number is the maximum number of rows of a shape encountered and the nesting number is the maximum number of columns. Conjugating every Young diagram in the vacillating tableau gives the symmetry. For complete matchings the construction specializes to oscillating tableaux and to lattice walks in Weyl chambers, giving explicit models and enumerations for k-noncrossing matchings. For the original problem, the most useful content is the dynamic tableau encoding of matchings/partitions, the way crossing information is converted into subsequence/order information, and the fixed-endpoint/canonical noncrossing constructions that may help organize induction or discrete Morse collapses on crossing resolutions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2 bijection ψ: vacillating tableaux and decorated set partitions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [n] = {1,...,n}. A vacillating tableau of shape λ and length 2n is a sequence λ_0, λ_1, ..., λ_{2n} of integer partitions such that λ_0 = ∅, λ_{2n} = λ, λ_{2i+1} is obtained from λ_{2i} by doing nothing or deleting one square, and λ_{2i} is obtained from λ_{2i−1} by doing nothing or adding one square. There is a bijection ψ from vacillating tableaux of shape λ and length 2n to pairs (P,T), where P is a set partition of [n] and T is a standard Young tableau of shape λ whose entries form a subset of max(P), the set of maximal elements of the blocks of P. In particular, when λ = ∅, this gives a bijection between set partitions of [n] and vacillating tableaux of empty shape and length 2n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given V = (∅ = λ_0, λ_1, ..., λ_{2n} = λ), recursively construct pairs (P_i,T_i), where P_i is a set of ordered pairs from [n] and T_i is an SYT of shape λ_i. Start with P_0 empty and T_0 empty. If λ_i = λ_{i−1}, set (P_i,T_i) = (P_{i−1},T_{i−1}). If λ_i strictly contains λ_{i−1}, then i = 2k for some k, set P_i = P_{i−1}, and obtain T_i from T_{i−1} by placing the entry k in the newly added square. If λ_i is strictly contained in λ_{i−1}, then i = 2k−1 for some k; use inverse row insertion to write T_{i−1} as T_i ← j for a uniquely determined integer j, and set P_i = P_{i−1} ∪ {(j,k)}. The construction ensures that each integer occurs at most once as a first coordinate and at most once as a second coordinate, and all edges increase, so P_{2n} is the standard representation of a set partition P into directed paths. Any entry left in T_{2n} cannot have an outgoing edge, hence is maximal in its block, giving content(T_{2n}) ⊆ max(P). Conversely, given (P,T), set T_{2n}=T and reconstruct backwards for k=n,n−1,...,1: delete k from T_{2k} if k appears to obtain T_{2k−1}, otherwise do nothing; then, if the standard representation of P has an edge (i,k), obtain T_{2k−2} by row-inserting i into T_{2k−1}, otherwise do nothing. These two procedures are inverse.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5: enumeration of vacillating tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let λ be an integer partition of k, let g_λ(n) be the number of vacillating tableaux of shape λ and length 2n, let f^λ be the number of standard Young tableaux of shape λ with content [k], and let B(n,k) be the number of set partitions of [n] with k distinguished blocks. Then g_λ(n) = B(n,k) f^λ. Moreover the exponential generating function of B(n,k) is Σ_{n≥0} B(n,k) x^n/n! = (1/k!)(e^x−1)^k exp(e^x−1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the bijection ψ. For a fixed set partition P, choosing the content of T is exactly choosing k maximal block elements, equivalently choosing k distinguished blocks of P; once those k contents are chosen, the number of SYT of shape λ on that ordered set is f^λ, because only the relative order matters. This gives g_λ(n)=B(n,k)f^λ. For the generating function, a partition with k distinguished blocks is obtained by splitting the label set into the union of elements lying in the distinguished blocks and elements lying in ordinary blocks, partitioning the first part into exactly k nonempty blocks and marking them, and partitioning the second part arbitrarily. The exponential generating function is therefore the product of the EGF for k unordered nonempty blocks, (e^x−1)^k/k!, and the Bell-number EGF exp(e^x−1).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 map φ: inverse construction from partitions to vacillating tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a set partition P of [n] with standard representation by arcs joining consecutive elements in each block, the inverse φ(P) of ψ can be constructed directly as follows. Set T_{2n}=∅ and scan j from n down to 1, defining T_{2j−1} and T_{2j−2}. If j is a right endpoint of an arc (i,j) but not a left endpoint, first do nothing and then row-insert i. If j is a left endpoint of an arc (j,k) but not a right endpoint, first delete j and then do nothing. If j is isolated, do nothing twice. If j is both a right endpoint of (i,j) and a left endpoint of (j,k), first delete j and then row-insert i. The sequence of shapes of the tableaux T_0,T_1,...,T_{2n} is the vacillating tableau φ(P).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the inverse procedure in the special case λ=∅ of the bijection ψ, written in a way that uses the four possible local statuses of j in the standard representation: right endpoint only, left endpoint only, isolated point, or internal point of a block. Scanning from right to left means that when a right endpoint j is encountered, the corresponding left endpoint i becomes active and is inserted into the tableau; when a left endpoint j is encountered, its active entry is removed. The RSK inverse/forward insertion rules ensure that this recovers exactly the tableaux that ψ would have produced.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6: crossing and nesting numbers from vacillating tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a set partition of [n], with standard representation consisting of arcs (i,j) between consecutive elements of each block. A k-crossing of P is a set of arcs (i_1,j_1),...,(i_k,j_k) such that i_1 < i_2 < ... < i_k < j_1 < j_2 < ... < j_k. A k-nesting is a set of arcs (i_1,j_1),...,(i_k,j_k) such that i_1 < i_2 < ... < i_k < j_k < ... < j_2 < j_1. Let cr(P) be the maximum k for which P has a k-crossing, and let ne(P) be the maximum k for which P has a k-nesting. If φ(P) = (∅ = λ_0, λ_1, ..., λ_{2n} = ∅), then cr(P) is the maximum, over all i, of the number of rows of λ_i, and ne(P) is the maximum, over all i, of the number of columns of λ_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let T(P) = (T_0,...,T_{2n}) be the sequence of SYT constructed by φ. An arc (i,j) of P is characterized by the fact that the entry i appears in exactly the tableaux T_{2i}, T_{2i+1}, ..., T_{2j−2}; the paper says that i is added at step i and leaves at step j. Thus arcs (i_1,j_1),...,(i_k,j_k) form a k-crossing exactly when at some common time the entries i_1<...<i_k are all present and they leave in increasing order of their values; they form a k-nesting exactly when at some common time they are all present and leave in decreasing order. For each tableau T_i, define a word σ_i recursively backwards: σ_{2n} is empty; if T_i=T_{i−1}, set σ_{i−1}=σ_i; if T_{i−1} is obtained from T_i by row-inserting j, set σ_{i−1}=σ_i j; and if T_i is obtained from T_{i−1} by adding entry i/2, delete i/2 from σ_i to obtain σ_{i−1}. Then σ_i is a permutation of content(T_i), and if σ_i=w_1...w_r, the entries leave T(P) in the order w_r,...,w_1. The key claim is that the RSK insertion tableau of σ_i is exactly T_i. This is proved by backward induction. The only nontrivial case is deleting the largest entry j from σ_i: writing σ_i = u_1...u_s j v_1...v_t with j larger than every other letter, one proves by induction on t that removing j from the word removes exactly the square containing j from the insertion tableau. The insertion-path analysis splits into whether j lies on the insertion path of v_t; if it does, j, being largest, is bumped only into the next row as a terminal entry, and if it does not, the same insertion path occurs in the tableau with j removed. Finally apply Schensted's theorem: for a word with distinct letters and insertion shape λ, the longest increasing subsequence has length equal to the number of columns of λ, and the longest decreasing subsequence has length equal to the number of rows of λ. Since crossings correspond to decreasing subsequences of some σ_i and nestings correspond to increasing subsequences of some σ_i, the result follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1: fixed-minimum/fixed-maximum symmetry of crossings and nestings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For subsets S,T of [n] with |S|=|T|, let P_n(S,T) be the set of set partitions P of [n] such that min(P)=S and max(P)=T, where min(P) and max(P) are respectively the sets of minimal and maximal elements of blocks of P. Let f_{n,S,T}(i,j) be the number of P in P_n(S,T) with cr(P)=i and ne(P)=j, where cr and ne are the maximum crossing and nesting sizes in the standard representation. Then f_{n,S,T}(i,j) = f_{n,S,T}(j,i) for all i,j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the bijection φ from P_n(S,T) to vacillating tableaux of empty shape. By Theorem 6, cr(P) is the maximum number of rows among all shapes in φ(P), and ne(P) is the maximum number of columns. Define an involution τ on vacillating tableaux by replacing every Young diagram λ_i with its conjugate λ_i′. This preserves the allowed vacillating moves, because adding, deleting, or not changing one square remains an allowed move after conjugation. It swaps the maximum number of rows and the maximum number of columns. The local move pattern around the pair of times 2i−2, 2i−1, 2i determines whether i is a left endpoint only, right endpoint only, isolated point, or internal point of a block; conjugation preserves whether the step is an addition, deletion, or equality, hence preserves min(P) and max(P). Therefore τ induces an involution of P_n(S,T) that interchanges cr and ne.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 2, 3, and 4: consequences for partitions and complete matchings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let NCN_{k,l}(n) be the number of set partitions of [n] that have no k-crossing and no l-nesting, equivalently cr(P)<k and ne(P)<l. Then NCN_{k,l}(n)=NCN_{l,k}(n). In particular, the number NC_k(n) of k-noncrossing partitions of [n] equals the number NN_k(n) of k-nonnesting partitions of [n]. For complete matchings on [2n], viewed as set partitions into n blocks of size 2, the same symmetry holds: for every pair of disjoint subsets S,T of [2n] with |S|=|T|=n, cr and ne have symmetric joint distribution on P_{2n}(S,T); summing over such S,T, the number of matchings with cr=i and ne=j equals the number with cr=j and ne=i; and the number of k-noncrossing matchings equals the number of k-nonnesting matchings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For set partitions, sum the equality f_{n,S,T}(i,j)=f_{n,S,T}(j,i) from Theorem 1 over all S,T and over the relevant ranges 1≤i<k and 1≤j<l. For complete matchings, observe that a partition of [2n] is a complete matching exactly when |min(P)|=|max(P)|=n and min(P)∩max(P)=∅, equivalently the chosen S and T are disjoint n-element subsets. Restrict Theorem 1 to those S,T and then sum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10: lattice-walk model for k-noncrossing partitions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k≥2 and let V_k = {(a_1,...,a_{k−1}) in Z^{k−1}: a_1 ≥ a_2 ≥ ... ≥ a_{k−1} ≥ 0}. The number of k-noncrossing set partitions of [n], meaning partitions P with cr(P)<k, equals the number of closed lattice walks of length 2n in V_k from the origin to itself with steps in {±ε_1,...,±ε_{k−1},0}, where ε_i is the ith unit coordinate vector, subject to the parity rule that after an even number of steps the walk either moves by −ε_i or stays still, and after an odd number of steps the walk either moves by +ε_i or stays still.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem 6, a partition is k-noncrossing exactly when every shape λ_i in φ(P) has at most k−1 rows. Record λ_i by its row-length vector (a_1,...,a_{k−1}), padding with zeroes. The Young-diagram condition is exactly a_1≥...≥a_{k−1}≥0. A vacillating deletion or addition changes exactly one coordinate by −1 or +1, and doing nothing gives the zero step. The definition of vacillating tableaux imposes the parity rule: odd-index intermediate shapes arise from doing nothing or deleting after an even time, and even-index shapes arise from doing nothing or adding after an odd time. The empty initial and final shapes give a closed walk from the origin to itself, and the construction is reversible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Fixed endpoint Motzkin-path characterization and canonical 2-noncrossing/2-nonnesting partitions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For subsets S,T of [n] with |S|=|T|, the set P_n(S,T) of partitions with min(P)=S and max(P)=T is nonempty if and only if |S∩[i]| ≥ |T∩[i]| for every i in [n]. Equivalently, define a lattice path L(S,T) from (0,0) to (2n,0) by reading i=1,...,n and taking two steps as follows: if i is in S∩T take (1,0),(1,0); if i is in S−T take (1,0),(1,1); if i is in T−S take (1,−1),(1,0); if i is in neither S nor T take (1,−1),(1,1). Then P_n(S,T) is nonempty if and only if L(S,T) is a Motzkin path, i.e. never goes below the x-axis. When L(S,T) is Motzkin, the paper gives explicit recovery of the unique noncrossing partition and the unique nonnesting partition with these min and max sets: write the path heights as a_0,...,a_{2n}, let A=[n]−T={i_1<...<i_t} and B=[n]−S={j_1<...<j_t}. The noncrossing standard representation pairs each j_r with max{i_s in A: i_s<j_r and a_{2i_s}=a_{2j_r−2}}, while the nonnesting standard representation pairs j_r with i_r for every r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The prefix inequality counts open blocks: among the first i labels, the number of block minima already seen must be at least the number of block maxima already seen. The path L(S,T) is just a height encoding of this open-block count with two half-steps for each label, so the same condition is that its height never becomes negative. Conversely, the displayed pairing rules construct standard representations with the prescribed left and right endpoint sets. Pairing a right endpoint to the most recent compatible left endpoint at the same height gives the stack-like noncrossing construction; pairing the rth available left endpoint to the rth right endpoint gives the queue-like nonnesting construction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8 and Corollary 9: nestings as decreasing subsequences and Greene sums
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a set partition whose standard representation has arcs (i_1,j_1),...,(i_t,j_t), ordered so that i_r<j_r for each r and j_1<j_2<...<j_t. Let α(P)=i_1 i_2 ... i_t be the word of left endpoints in increasing order of right endpoints. Then there is a one-to-one correspondence between nestings of P and decreasing subsequences of α(P). Define the nesting graph Ne(P) to have these arcs as vertices, with two vertices adjacent when the corresponding arcs are nested. Let ne_r(P) be the maximum number of vertices contained in a union of r cliques of Ne(P), equivalently the maximum number of arcs contained in a union of r nestings. If λ is the RSK shape of α(P) and λ′ is its conjugate, then ne_r(P)=λ′_1+...+λ′_r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the tableau process T(P), the word α(P) records the order in which entries leave, by increasing right endpoint. Let σ be the reverse word i_t...i_1. In the proof of Theorem 6, nestings are represented by increasing subsequences of the auxiliary words σ_i associated to tableaux T_i. Since each σ_i is a subsequence of σ, every nesting gives an increasing subsequence of σ, equivalently a decreasing subsequence of α(P). Conversely, if i_{r_1}<...<i_{r_m} is an increasing subsequence of σ, then, because the terms leave in reverse order, all of them are simultaneously present just before the first of them leaves; hence they form a nesting. The corollary then applies Greene's extension of Schensted's theorem: for a word of RSK shape λ, the largest size of a union of r decreasing subsequences is λ′_1+...+λ′_r.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 11: enhanced crossing/nesting symmetry via hesitating tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition P of [n], its enhanced representation is the standard representation together with a loop (i,i) at each isolated point i. An enhanced k-crossing is a set of enhanced edges (i_1,j_1),...,(i_k,j_k) such that i_1<...<i_k≤j_1<...<j_k; an enhanced k-nesting is a set such that i_1<...<i_k≤j_k<...<j_1. Let cr_en(P) and ne_en(P) be the maximum enhanced crossing and nesting sizes. A hesitating tableau of empty shape and length 2n is a path in Young's lattice from ∅ to ∅ whose paired moves are one of: do nothing then add a square, delete a square then do nothing, or add a square then delete a square. For disjoint subsets S,T of [n] with |S|=|T|, let Pbar_n(S,T) be the set of partitions P with min(P)−max(P)=S and max(P)−min(P)=T. If fbar_{n,S,T}(i,j) counts P in Pbar_n(S,T) with cr_en(P)=i and ne_en(P)=j, then fbar_{n,S,T}(i,j)=fbar_{n,S,T}(j,i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper modifies φ to a bijection φbar from partitions to hesitating tableaux. As before, scan j from n down to 1 and use RSK insertion/deletion. If j is a left endpoint only or right endpoint only, use the same rules as φ. If j is isolated, first insert j and then delete j. If j is both a right endpoint of (i,j) and a left endpoint of (j,k), first insert i and then delete j. This changes the tableau model so that isolated points and adjacent arcs are counted in the enhanced inequalities. The same Schensted argument identifies cr_en with the maximum number of rows and ne_en with the maximum number of columns among the hesitating-tableau shapes. Conjugating all shapes swaps rows and columns. In the hesitating setting, conjugation preserves the statuses left endpoint only and right endpoint only, hence preserves min(P)−max(P) and max(P)−min(P), but it need not distinguish isolated points from internal points; this is why the theorem fixes S and T in that form.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5 specialization: complete matchings and oscillating tableaux; Corollaries 12 and 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a complete matching on [2m], applying φ and deleting all do-nothing steps yields an oscillating tableau of empty shape and length 2m, namely a sequence ∅=λ_0,λ_1,...,λ_{2m}=∅ in which consecutive shapes differ by adding or deleting exactly one square. This is a bijection between complete matchings on [2m] and oscillating tableaux of empty shape and length 2m. Consequently, for k≥2, the number of k-noncrossing matchings on [2m] equals the number of closed lattice walks of length 2m in V_k = {(a_1,...,a_{k−1}) in Z^{k−1}: a_1≥...≥a_{k−1}≥0} from the origin to itself with unit steps ±ε_i. In particular, 2-noncrossing matchings are in bijection with Dyck paths of length 2m, and 3-noncrossing matchings are in bijection with pairs of noncrossing Dyck paths with common endpoints.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In a complete matching every vertex is either a left endpoint only or a right endpoint only, so each two-step vacillating move has exactly one nontrivial add/delete operation. Removing the trivial operations gives an oscillating tableau, and the construction is reversible by reinserting the forced do-nothing moves according to endpoint type. The row-length encoding used in Theorem 10 now has no zero steps and no parity restriction, giving walks in V_k with steps ±ε_i. For k=2 this is a one-dimensional nonnegative walk from 0 to 0 with up/down unit steps, i.e. a Dyck path. For k=3, a walk (x_i,y_i) with x_i≥y_i≥0 is sent to two Dyck paths P_i=x_i+y_i and Q_i=x_i−y_i; the inequalities ensure P stays weakly above Q, and the construction is invertible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5 enumeration formulas for k-noncrossing and bounded crossing/nesting matchings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f_k(m) be the number of k-noncrossing complete matchings on [2m], and define the exponential generating function F_k(x)=Σ_m f_k(m) x^{2m}/(2m)!. Then F_k(x)=det[I_{i−j}(2x)−I_{i+j}(2x)]_{i,j=1}^{k−1}, where I_m(2x)=Σ_{j≥0} x^{m+2j}/(j!(m+j)!) is the hyperbolic Bessel function of the first kind. In particular F_2(x)=Σ_{j≥0} C_j x^{2j}/(2j)!, where C_j is the jth Catalan number, and f_3(m)=3!(2m+2)!/(m!(m+1)!(m+2)!(m+3)!)=C_m C_{m+2}−C_{m+1}^2. More generally, if g_{k,j}(m) is the number of complete matchings on [2m] with no (k+1)-crossing and no (j+1)-nesting, then g_{k,j}(m) is the number of length 2m walks from ∅ to ∅ in the Hasse diagram of L(k,j), the poset of Young diagrams fitting in a k by j rectangle. Its ordinary generating function G_{k,j}(x)=Σ_m g_{k,j}(m)x^{2m} satisfies G_{k,j}(x)=det(I−x A_{k,j}(0))/det(I−x A_{k,j}), where A_{k,j} is the adjacency matrix of the Hasse diagram of L(k,j), and A_{k,j}(0) is obtained by deleting the row and column corresponding to ∅.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The determinant formula for F_k is obtained by combining the oscillating-tableau/lattice-walk correspondence with the Grabiner-Magyar determinant for walks in Weyl chambers, specialized to walks from the origin back to itself in the chamber V_k. The k=2 and k=3 evaluations are algebraic simplifications of that determinant and match the Catalan and noncrossing-Dyck-pair counts. For simultaneous crossing and nesting bounds, Theorem 6 says that all shapes must remain inside a k by j rectangle. The transfer matrix method for walks on the finite graph given by the Hasse diagram of L(k,j) gives the stated ratio of determinants for the return-walk generating function.

proof_techniques.name: Right-to-left RSK encoding of open arcs
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes a set partition by scanning labels from right to left and maintaining an SYT of currently open left endpoints. Encountering a right endpoint (i,j) inserts i; encountering a left endpoint j deletes j; isolated and internal points are handled by the corresponding combination of do-nothing, deletion, and insertion. This turns a static arc diagram into a time sequence of tableaux, with the shape recording a controlled amount of global crossing/nesting information.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original matching complex, one can try to encode a matching or a partial resolution by the set/order of arcs crossing a moving vertical line. The RSK tableau may supply a complexity measure for induction or a canonical choice of crossing to resolve.

proof_techniques.name: Crossings and nestings as departure-order subsequences
proof_techniques.description: A central observation is that a family of arcs forms a crossing exactly when their left endpoints are simultaneously active and leave in increasing order, while it forms a nesting when they are simultaneously active and leave in decreasing order. The auxiliary words σ_i record active entries in reverse departure order, so crossings become decreasing subsequences and nestings become increasing subsequences. Schensted's theorem then converts these order conditions into row and column bounds on Young diagrams.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests analyzing crossings not only geometrically but as inversions in the order in which paired endpoints become inactive. Resolving a crossing may be interpretable as altering a departure order, which could support a monotone induction or discrete Morse matching.

proof_techniques.name: Shape conjugation involution
proof_techniques.description: After translating partitions into vacillating tableaux, conjugating every Young diagram preserves the valid move sequence and the endpoint-status pattern, while swapping rows and columns. This gives an involution preserving fixed min/max endpoint data and exchanging crossing and nesting statistics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original word constraints can be expressed as fixed endpoint types or colors, a similar shape-level involution or symmetry might preserve admissibility while exchanging two complementary complexity measures. Even if not directly used, it indicates that endpoint-preserving transformations can be easier after passing to tableau paths.

proof_techniques.name: Weyl-chamber lattice-walk model
proof_techniques.description: Bounding the crossing number translates into bounding the number of rows of every tableau shape. Recording row lengths gives a walk in the chamber a_1≥...≥a_{k−1}≥0, with allowed steps determined by add/delete/stay moves. For complete matchings, this simplifies to oscillating tableaux and walks with unit coordinate steps.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Subfamilies of matchings with controlled crossing behavior may be modeled as walks in a finite or locally finite chamber. Such a model can make induction on first exit, maximal height, or return structure more systematic.

proof_techniques.name: Fixed-endpoint Motzkin paths and canonical noncrossing/nonnesting objects
proof_techniques.description: For fixed min and max sets S,T, nonemptiness is equivalent to a Motzkin prefix condition. When nonempty, the paper gives explicit stack-like and queue-like pairings that recover the unique noncrossing and unique nonnesting partitions with those endpoint sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a fixed reducible word, admissible pairings of inverse letters may have analogous prefix conditions and canonical noncrossing representatives. Such representatives are natural candidates for deformation targets or base vertices in a contractibility proof.

proof_techniques.name: Enhanced crossings via hesitating tableaux
proof_techniques.description: The paper modifies the vacillating-tableau construction to include loops at isolated points and to count enhanced crossings/nestings, where inequalities allow i_k≤j_1 or i_k≤j_k. The resulting hesitating tableaux use paired moves of types stay-add, delete-stay, and add-delete, and the same RSK/Schensted/conjugation strategy applies with slightly different preserved endpoint data.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original quasi-reduced condition is a local constraint on adjacent inverse patterns; enhanced crossing ideas may be useful if local cancellations or shared-endpoint analogues need to be represented in the combinatorics of resolutions.

proof_techniques.name: Finite transfer-matrix reduction under simultaneous row and column bounds
proof_techniques.description: When both crossing and nesting sizes are bounded, all tableau shapes lie in a fixed rectangle, so the problem becomes counting closed walks in a finite Hasse diagram. The paper applies the transfer matrix method to express return-walk generating functions as determinant ratios.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof reduces to bounded subcomplexes or finite intervals in a poset of resolutions, the same finite-state viewpoint may help compute examples, identify recurrences, or verify collapses in small cases.

other_useful_info: Notation caveat: in this paper a k-crossing means a family of k mutually crossing arcs with endpoints i_1<...<i_k<j_1<...<j_k, and cr is the maximum size of such a family; it is not the total number of intersection points in a drawn matching. The paper notes that usual noncrossing partitions are exactly partitions with no 2-crossing in the standard representation, and usual nonnesting partitions are exactly those with no 2-nesting. It also notes that its notion of k-noncrossing partitions differs from stronger notions such as Klazar's for k≥3. The authors explicitly state that they do not have a Greene-theorem analogue for crossing graphs comparable to their nesting result, because an increasing subsequence of the left-endpoint word gives a crossing only under the extra condition that the last left endpoint lies before the first right endpoint. Related cited tools include Sundaram's oscillating-tableau correspondence, Grabiner-Magyar Weyl-chamber walk enumeration, and Baik-Rains/Goulden determinant and asymptotic results for matchings and fixed-point-free involutions.
why_relevant: Provides detailed combinatorics of crossing and nesting statistics for matchings. These techniques can help organize induction on crossing number and understand how resolving crossings changes matching structure.
